results.title: Copresent - Remote Control for Google Slides - Chrome Web Store
results.content: Control Google Slides together — no more "next slide please." One person installs the extension; everyone else presents from a single shared link, on any device. Copresent is the simplest way to present Google Slides as a team. Think of it as a shared remote for Google Slides — everyone holds the clicker. Multiple presenters can advance slides, see the live current slide, and read their speaker notes from an iPhone, Android, iPad, or laptop. Only the main presenter installs anything; co-presenters join through a secure link that works instantly in any browser. No downloads, no accounts, no extra apps. KEY FEATURES 🖼️ See the current slide live in your remote — no need to watch the projector 📱 Turn your phone into a remote for Google Slides — swipe to advance, no clicker needed 📝 Read your [...] 💼 Investor pitches — co-founders take turns with zero handoff fumbles 🏫 Town halls & all-hands — multiple speakers, one screen share 🎓 Lectures & workshops — roam the room, control slides from your phone 🎥 Remote meetings — present over Zoom, Google Meet & Microsoft Teams 🎤 Solo presenting — just want a phone clicker and your notes? That works too ABOUT COPRESENT Copresent is the easiest way to co-present and remote-control Google Slides with a team — on stage, in the boardroom, or fully remote. One install, a shared link, and you're in sync. Works with Google Slides. Set up in under 30 seconds. [...] Slides — swipe to advance, no clicker needed 📝 Read your speaker notes on every device 🤼 Up to 10 presenters control one deck, taking turns 🔗 One shared link — co-presenters join instantly, no sign-up 🎥 Works over Zoom, Google Meet & Microsoft Teams 🆓 Free, and set up in under 30 seconds 🖼️ Live Slide Preview 🖼️ - Co-presenters see the current slide, rendered live, right in their remote - Always know what's on screen — from the back of the room or a second monitor - Stays in sync even when your presenting window is in the background - Turn it on by connecting your Google account (optional) 📱 Your Phone, a Google Slides Remote 📱 - Swipe left and right to move through your Google Slides - Remote-control your slides from the stage or the back of the room — no physical clicker needed - Works

results.title: App-pocalypse Now
results.content:
## The app user experience is wildly inconsistent

Have you ever tried actually using the Amazon app on iOS, Android, and Windows? iOS does the best, mostly because it’s been an app platform for longer than the others, but even there, the Amazon app is a frustrating morass of missing and incomplete functions from the website. Sure, maybe you don’t need the full breadth of Amazon functions on your phone, though that’s debatable on a tablet. But natural web conveniences like opening links in new tabs, sharing links, the back button, searching within the page, and zooming in and out are available inconsistently, if at all. [...] Have you ever noticed that the people complaining about apps that cost $3.99 are the same people dropping five bucks on a cup of fancy coffee without batting an eyelash? Me too, and I’m with the coffee people. $3.99 for your app? Outraaageous! [...] For the sake of argument, let’s say the app is free, and the developers are ethical, so you trust that they won’t do anything sketchy with the personal information on your device to make ends meet. Great! But they still have to make a living, don’t they? Which means doing anything useful in the app requires buying three “optional” add-ons that cost $2.99 each. Or there are special fees for performing certain actions. Isn’t this stuff you would want to know before installing the app? You betcha. Maybe the app is properly tagged as “offering in-app purchases” but the entire burden of discovering exactly what “in-app purchases” means, and how much the app will ultimately cost you, is placed completely on your shoulders. You, the poor, bedraggled user.

results.title: Topic: Why do apps freeze or crash | Novocraft
results.content:
To fix the issue, try these steps:

  Restart your device (it’s the tech equivalent of a reset button for your brain).  
   Close other apps to free up resources.  
   Update the app and your operating system.  
   Clear the app’s cache or reinstall it if the issue persists.  
   Check your device’s storage and make some space if it’s almost full.  
   Freezing and crashing can be frustrating, but usually, there’s a solution. And hey, if all else fails, you can reach out to the app’s support team—they’re there to help!
 Author

#### LATEST NEWS [...] Network issues: Many apps rely on internet connectivity. If your Wi-Fi or mobile data is unstable, apps that depend on the network can freeze while trying to fetch data.

  Too many apps running: Multitasking is great, but having a bunch of apps open can hog system resources, causing your device to slow down or apps to crash.

  Corrupted app files: Sometimes, an app’s installation or update process doesn’t go smoothly, leading to corrupted files that make it unstable.

  To fix the issue, try these steps: [...] #
